Today we're going to talk about graphic design. By the time we're done today you're going to have a whole toolkit of graphics tools and rules that will help boost your response in print and on the web. By print I mean both direct mail and print ads and so forth. I want to start by saying I never wanted to learn anything about graphic design. I always figured that the blood, sweat and tears that I invested in my copy should be enough. It should be enough to give me big winners and make me a lot of money. But after years of watching my copy get slaughtered by artists who didn't understand what it takes to grab attention and to keep people reading very dense, long copy promotions, I had to begin learning about graphics really in self-defense. My son-in-law is an artist, a graphic designer, and I have great, and my best friend, and I have great affinity to graphics people. But what happens a lot is that the people who get into graphic design do it for one of two reasons. Number one, they want to make the world a prettier place, they're artists. Number two, they're a large group of them that have no training but they understand how to use the software and they like working with computers. And so I found myself continually working with artists to teach these principles. And I'm excited about today's session because now I'm just going to be able to give the artists an MP3 before I start working with them and hopefully I'll be able to avoid a lot of grits that cost time and effort down the road. Graphics are important to copywriters for so many reasons, but if your copywriter, poor design, can cut your response rate, it can adversely affect your average unit of sale, and if it does that, the copy you've worked so hard on for so long could fail to beat the control, in which case you get no royalties. And as we've discussed so many times, a royalty on a good direct mail piece or on a good web campaign can easily be in the six figures. And so graphic design, if not properly executed, could basically eliminate that six figures from your annual income this year. And every single time you run into that kind of situation. On the other hand, if you have great design, you can get a higher hit rate and again add many hundreds of thousands to your income if you're a copywriter. And needless to say, if you're a business owner or a marketing exec, the difference between good design and weaker design can mean millions of dollars a year in sales. And the reason for that is because graphics have two essential jobs to do on every promotion. The first one is to capture attention. If you don't capture your prospect's attention, he's not going to read the copy, you're not going to make the sale. And sending copy out like we provide it, as copywriters provide it in 12-point times Roman, is not going to capture attention. And secondly, the purpose of graphics is to promote readership of the entire sales message. And that's done by the Judicial Selection of Type and Layout and all of the other things we're going to look at today. So this is the kind of thing that for all of us, everyone on this call can stand to make a lot of money understanding these basics. And in these basic principles, good graphics matter. I have personally seen poorly designed promo cut ROI by as much as 60%. The damage bad graphics can do is huge. It's bigger than the improvement they can provide actually from the mean. And the reason for that is that it's possible for bad graphics to make your promotion virtually invisible in the mailbox and impossible to read. And the harder you make your prospects work in order to get your sales message, the fewer people, the lower your response is going to be. And also there's something to say for corporate identity. If the graphics look, make the piece look as if it were just thrown together, it reflects poorly on the quality of your company. And that's especially important in B2B sales. On the other hand, I've seen well-designed promotions increase response from the mean by as much as 30%. So you have a large range there with good graphics way to heck up here and bad graphics down here. Again, another reason why today's session is so essential and why these tools are so valuable for both copywriters as well as business owners and marketers. Good and bad graphics are not really subjective terms in our industry. The thing is when it comes to art, art is very subjective. I've been to the National Institute of Modern Art and I've seen stuff that the third grade class with a couple of cans of paint could have done. And it's been labeled as good art or even genius by the reviewers. But in our industry, it's not subjective. In our industry, everything, whether the definition of good or bad comes down to, did it increase response or did it lower response? Good graphics can be particularly powerful in helping you seize your prospects' attention, converting that attention to readership. We've often talked about the deck copy and we'll look at several other ways that good graphics can help convert attention to readership today. Good graphics keep your prospect reading. They help your copy. We've talked about how each paragraph you write is a salesman for the next paragraph. And the good news is, although that's still true, good graphics are another salesman that draw people's eyes down into the copy and cause them to read your sales message. So your copy doesn't have to do it alone. Good graphics will grab scanner's eyeballs and drive them into the copy. We'll look at ways that they do that today, primarily through illustrations and sidebars and other devices. Good graphics can also help dimensionalize your selling arguments. If you're trying to make a point, I write a lot of promotions, for example, in the financial markets. And if you're writing a promotion and you're trying to convince people that the dollars are at an all-time low and that foreign stocks are at an all-time high, you can present a simple chart and source it to the Federal Reserve Board and you have instant credibility for your argument. So in that way, not only can good graphics, the use of graphics, dimensionalize your selling argument, they can also reduce the amount of space you have to dedicate in your text to making that point because it's right there. Picture's worth a thousand words. Good graphics, dimensionalize product benefits. Instead of just saying that a particular vitamin product will reverse heart disease or take plaque out of your arteries, you can show pictures of it. And again, the picture's worth a thousand words and really drives the point home on a visceral level how the product works and the benefits it provides. We often use graphics to dimensionalize the horrifying alternative. What happens if you don't buy this product? What happens if you continue going the way you're going now? And we can use photographs, illustrations, charts, graphs and other things to drive that point home on a visceral level. Graphics can be used to intensify resident emotions that you're counting on to help you make the sale. Emotion's your prospect already has it. Just if they're only activated, it almost makes your sale an impulse sale because they're already there. So long as they're experiencing that emotion, they're ready to buy. And I'll show you a funny cover that I did once for Martin Weiss that did that quite well. Good graphics also help you dimensionalize value. There was a promotion several years ago, and I couldn't find it for this webinar, but there was a promotion in Gorda several years ago where they actually had a mathematical equation on the page facing your order form where they showed the regular price, they showed – they started out showing what other people charged for a similar product, then they showed their regular price, then they showed their discount price, then they added in all of their premiums, the value of all of the free gifts that you were getting, and then brought it down at the bottom line to how much you save, how much you pay, and then divided that by the number of days per year so it's only X number of pennies per day. So while we do that kind of thing in our running copy, you can also do it with graphics and drive the point home in a much more forceful way. And finally, good graphics help make ordering easy and do what we all are looking to do in our promotions, which is to make every order an impulse order so the prospect really doesn't have to think about it or set the promotion aside and revisit it later. So that's an awful lot of good things that graphics can do for you. It constantly amazes me that so many direct marketers, especially direct mailers, put very little stock in the quality of their graphics and are willing to try to save a couple of thousand dollars by going with an artist who really doesn't understand the power of graphics. And in many cases, I believe that they're leaving a lot of money on the table every year. That's to me the epitome of being pennywise and pound foolish. All right, good graphics do all of the things we just talked about by enhancing visibility and intention getting power of your piece, making the pages and spreads feel inviting to the prospect. This has to do with really just when you open a magazine, does the spread draw you in or does it look like a reading assignment? Does it look like it's going to be dense and hard to get through? Now we deal with long copy and long copy can be intimidating. So good graphics, and we'll look at some of these techniques today, good graphics make that spread, even though it's dense with copy, make it look inviting and easy to read. Good graphics eliminate visual barriers. We'll look at some of them today, for example, full height sidebars on right-hand pages, vertical rules between columns of text, things that subliminally communicate that I've just hit the wall here and I really don't have to continue reading. Good graphics make copies easy to read. We talked about that. They create an easy to follow eye path so that the prospect, the reader, never has to wonder where he's supposed to look next. If you leave the decision up to them quite often, they'll make the wrong decision rather than make, they'll decide not to keep reading rather than decide to turn the page or decide to move from point A to point B in your copy. And it's important to us as direct marketers because a lot of our sales arguments are linear in nature. You begin with a basic proposition and then you move to the next step and the next step, each step leading you inextricably to the conclusion that you'd have to be an idiot not to buy this product. So eye path is very important for us. Good graphics create compelling page turners. And one time, for example, in a promotion I did for Boardroom, I made sure that on the lower right hand corner of every spread, I essentially had a table of contents for the next spread. And there would be three or four fascinations. What you'll learn if you turn this page. Powerful page turner, right? Well, a good artist knows how to use that to keep the prospect reading. And we talked about how good graphics will create compelling proof elements. Anyone could create a chart or a graph or an illustration. Anybody can get a stock photograph. But a good graphic artist knows how to use these things in the context of the copy for the greatest impact. We'll look at some of those today. Good graphics also establish eye contact with the prospect. When you're going to a prospect and wanting to establish, wanting him as a customer, what you're really looking for is a way to establish a relationship with this guy. You want to look him in the eye. You want him to look you in the eye. You want to establish a basis for trust and a basis for this new relationship. And so, as we'll see here, many direct mailers, and I especially, make sure that I establish eye contact between my advocate, my spokesperson, and the prospect on every spread. Excuse me. Good graphics also do it by the proper use of color psychology. And on this last one, see, I found a typo after I uploaded this. But effective use of sidebars and overall spread layout in order to take people who were scanners like me and grab them by the eyeballs and draw them down into the copy. So those are an awful lot of benefits that good graphics offer and make understanding how they work even very, very important. Now, to do all of those things, you have a lot of things in your graphics toolbox. You can choose a format that's appropriate for the subject in the market. You can choose from various page sizes, type faces, type sizes, line lengths, paragraph formatting, bullets, colors, sidebars, backgrounds, illustrations, readership devices. We'll look at some of these action devices, guarantees, value devices, response devices. We're going to look at some of all of these today and talk a little bit about the philosophy behind them. All right. I came up with 30 things that I want to talk to you about today in terms of graphics. There are more and we'll do other calls, more advanced calls later on, but I think today will be an excellent foundation for you. The first thing a graphic artist has to do, and fortunately other people have input into this as well, is to get the format right for your promotion piece. Right now, if you're in the mail, for example, you have a lot of choices. You could go with a large tabloid. You could go with an 8.5 by 11 format piece. All of these, by the way, were 24 pages long. You could go with an 8.5 by 11 format piece. This size could be used well as a mag-a-log, a simulated magazine, or in a special report format that looks a little more serious and a little less consensual. It's more sumerish, more serious as we do quite often in the financial markets. You can go with a jumbo envelope package, as I did for Garzarelli and Louis Nabilier. These are custom size packages. There's something I want to mention on that in a moment. You can go with book-a-logs. We find book-a-logs are working really well for us right now in the mail. They go up to 64 pages in length. They're 5.5 by 8.5 in size, basically a half a sheet of 8.5 by 11 paper folded over. They're working as well for us as these big tabloids, which is good news because these big tabloids, these flats like tabloids and mag-a-logs are about to be subject to huge postal rate increases in the next few weeks. We're going to be defaulting to more book-a-logs. The book-a-logs are great because they have perceived value. You can't read it quite often up here in the very small type. We'll actually have a price printed on the book-a-log. This is obviously for advertorial purposes. They work very well. They're easy to produce quickly. They're great. 6x9 component packages are good for sales letter-based promotions as our number 10s. Of course, the variety is endless. Both of these envelope packages here are non-personalized. There's a window where the address along with the facing marks are printed on a response device, but nothing else in the package bears the prospect's name. We've all seen promotions where every component is personalized and actually looks like somebody sat down and typed this out on his Handysmith Corona behind his desk. When choosing the format, there are several considerations. The first one is that it should be the right fit for the message. If you're sending out something that's very, very urgent and time-sensitive, it probably shouldn't be in something that looks like it took a long time to design. You could put it out in a special report format with much less graphics than this, for example, or you could put it out in a letter format. But if the message itself is very time-sensitive, you don't want to go with a highly designed look. It should be the right fit for the market. Right now in the financial markets, the format of choice, and this may change in a few weeks when postal rates go up, but in the financial markets, the special report format piece is the format of choice. It seems to work better than the other formats people have tried. In the health market, on the other hand, the big tabloid, we call it the biggest, fattest, happiest, flusy in the whole biker bar, because it's lots of colors, lots of stock photographs, lots of lifestyle shots. This has tended to be our best-working piece, although, as I said, our book logs are starting to work just as well. You need to select a format that gives you an adequate page size for the graphics that you anticipate using. If you're going to have a lot of sidebars on each spread, you need a larger page size, and that's another reason why the tabloids work so well for us. But think about the page size because that's part of it. Look at what your competitors are using. That's often a really good clue because, especially if they're direct response savvy, they're testing their promotions and they're probably testing formats as well. So take a look at that. Also, cost is a consideration. The envelope package, especially the custom-sized envelope packages like this Jumbo, can be more costly because there are other fees involved. Every component needs to be folded. They need to be inserted and so forth. And if you have a custom envelope, especially, that can be an added expense. And finally, consider turnaround time. Now, a lot of my clients are really on a run-and-gun schedule. They will drop a mailing on January 1st or actually December 26th, scheduled to hit in January with all of their test panels in it. They'll sit there and look at their response for two weeks, which is roughly their doubling date. Then they'll make a determination on whether or not to roll out, and if so, buy how much. And then they immediately begin planning the next mailing. So the amount, number of new customers they bring in per year and therefore their profits are directly determined by how quickly they can get this turnaround done. And I found out, to my own dismay, when I did this package for Phillips, for Elaine Garzarelli's newsletter, and also for Louis Navalier's newsletter, we did a Jumbo. I found out to my own chagrin that the time it takes to produce Jumbo packages adds several weeks per mailing cycle to your schedule, which over the period of the year meant there were several less mailings we were able to do, which meant I made less money. And it also meant the client brought in fewer new customers. So they were effective, they worked well, good response rates, but that is a downside to this. Now, perhaps because the promotion looks different from these other formats people are used to, you might get an increase in response that offsets that, but take that into account as well. Number two, we're going to kind of mix the web and direct mail today a little bit. I hope that's okay. I'm going to be doing a series entirely on web graphics later on, but I thought this was interesting and something that might be able to help you out. When we first launched the Total Package, we were publishing it at a 600-pixel width, the width of the page. And what that did, I liked narrow columns for the inclusion of ads and links and quotations from the article or charts illustrating something I say in the article. And so I knew I wanted a narrow column. But the problem was, and this isn't the 600-pixel width one, this one I think is 800, but what we found was that that narrow column came out to almost half the page width. And yet all my web designers were telling me, oh, you can't go bigger than 600 pixels because people won't be able to see it on their computer screen and I have to scroll side to side to see your page. But it made no sense to me because that might have been what was true when they were in design school, but the fact of the matter is that every year that goes by people get wider and wider screens. And so we did two things. First of all, we did a poll to our subscriber base and asked them all to tell us what their screen resolution was. And the bottom line was nobody had less than 1200. So we felt really comfortable at going to 800 pixels and later 856 pixels. And it really airs out the page. It gives you a nice wide column for your main content and a good narrow column for the other content that you have on the page. Type selection is all important. In print, we've been conditioned by years of reading newspapers and most magazines to be able to quickly identify serif typefaces. Typefaces with these little flourishes and so forth on them. And it's generally black ink on white paper. We'll talk a little bit about contrast in a moment. On the web, though, those little things get all pixelated and they're more difficult to read. So you'll notice when you go to websites that most of them are a sans serif face, a good strong one like for Dan Ariel, Helvetica, or Tahoma. And so that's a major difference between the two mediums. Now size is important. There are some people who have done okay with serif faces. And I think Gary Halbert even uses Currier on his newsletters or used to. But size matters. If you're using a larger size, then it's less important which face you use, serif or sans serif, on the web. But this is just a really good, safe, general rule of thumb. You can't go wrong on the web using sans serif faces. And then both on the web and in direct mail, heads and subheads should contrast with the face you've selected for your body copy. And we'll talk about the colors of the heads and subheads, too. I quite often will use impacts for my heads and subheads in print pieces along with the Times Roman for the body copy. Tony just wrote in and asked, if I use Times New Roman for direct mail body copy and Ariel for website, what font do you recommend I use for headlines and subheads for both mediums? Tony, for me, I like to go with a good, strong sans serif face for my heads and subheads. And most of my pieces, if you've seen my swipe files, you'll notice that I use impact or something very close to impact for my heads and subheads. Also, in print, when you're doing a piece that has sidebars in it, I tend to go, although my running text, which is very long, tends to be in Times Roman, I may go with a helvetica face in the sidebars, in the body copy in the sidebars. And the reason for that is, if they're reading the main text, I don't really want them to be diverted to the sidebar. I want them to keep reading the text. The purpose of my sidebars is to convince people to get back into the text, to draw the scanners in. So I want to make sure that the running text in my sidebars is never larger to the eye than the running text in the main part of the article. And using helvetica there would be okay as well. Heads and subheads should generally be in bulk, but if you're using impact, don't ever try to plump that up. Because if you do, it closes up the little holes in the E's and the A's and makes it difficult to read. Impact is a naturally bold face. So a lot of this, we're going to be talking about type sizes in a moment, a lot of this is you have to just use good judgment because 12-point in one type face, for example, is smaller than 12-point in another type face. So it's not as simple as just saying always use 12-point or always use bold. There are type faces that are naturally bold and they're fine the way they are. Other type faces, you're going to want to make more bold in your heads and subheads. And Dan just asked, do I recommend different font style for letters as opposed to maglots and other formats? Yeah, I do. The thing is that you begin with a format and you say, what am I trying to tell my prospect with this format? And if you're doing a sales letter, you're trying to tell him this communication is more personal or more urgent because that didn't take time to design it and make it look all fancy. This is a more intimate, more personal, more urgent communication. And so we've decided on using this sales letter format. Well, if you've decided to go that way, what are you most likely to see a sales letter in? And there's something interesting happening here. Obviously, in the old days up until a few years ago, whenever we received a letter from anybody, it was in a courier type face, in a typewriter typeface, like courier or American typewriter. And all of us are conditioned to expect letters, sales letters as well as other letters, to be in that typeface. Even though today when you get a letter from somebody, chances are they did it on the computer and it's going to be in Times-Roman. But our expectation is that personal intimate communications when typed are going to be in a typewriter typeface. So the type selection should be driven to that degree by your selection of format, your selection of medium. Any of you who have been in this business for any period of time have heard the prohibition about using reverse type. I mean, it's even in David Ogilvy's book, Never Use Reverse Type. I don't believe in that prohibition and I break it with great regularity, but very carefully. And here's why. First of all, on the web, well, first of all, again, size matters. And as I wrote a few weeks ago, the Hollywood sign is 65-foot-high white letters against a dark hillside. That's reverse. Is it readable? Sure. Stop signs or white letters on a red background. Are they readable? Sure they are. Why? Because the type is large enough so that the fact that they're reversed actually increases their impact, actually makes them more readable. So you can play with this a little bit in terms of headlines and larger texts. And you'll see if you go through my swipe file or see any of the samples that I posted, my own work online, you're going to see a lot of examples, especially on covers, where I'll use a black cover with yellow headline type. But the type is 72-point type or larger. I've gone 120-point type. So if you're using a larger size type, pretty much all bets are off because it's going to be readable. But when you're talking about body text, body copy, the rule that we've all learned really is true. Black type on a white background is the most readable there is. And if you put anything on a dark background, it better not be serif type. It better not have these little flourishes because they totally get lost in print on a black background. So go with the black type on the white background as you rule with them and only break that rule for very short blocks of very large type. Sometimes breaking a rule can produce huge results. In newspapers, for example, if you look at a newspaper spread with several ads on it, you're going to see that most of the ads do not use reverse type. So if you do use reverse type, just for your headline, chances are you're going to give it a lot more attention and readership. I'd like to point out that there is a difference, though. You'll notice in what you're seeing online right now that I'm using white text on a darker background, on a red background. If you downloaded the PDF for today's session, you saw a white background with black and red text on it. And there's a difference between the two mediums. PowerPoint is automatically set up to do reverse typefaces. And that's because when you're looking at a page, you're looking at your television screen, you're looking at a monitor, you're looking at a gun that's shooting light into your eye. And if the entire page is white and it's shooting all that white into your eye, it can make it more difficult to read the darker text. Whereas on paper, it's just the opposite. And so differentiate between the two. And look at when you're doing PowerPoints, you're doing a webinar like this. Even when you're designing a website, you can get away with more reverse work than you could in print. And again, ultimately, you're the judge. If you're paying attention to your graphic design and you're aware of this rule, you can break it very carefully from time to time, but only when you look at something and say, you know, this is actually more readable in reverse than it is, you know, with the dark on the light background. Many of us, I especially, many of us are marketing to older people. Now, we're all used to seeing nine point type in newspapers, nine point times usually. We're all used to seeing type as small as seven and a half points on the web. If you go to Amazon.com or eBay or any of those sites, you're going to see a lot of seven and a half point sans serif type. But they're not selling with long copy. The readership is not as important to them as it is to us because we've got a lot more text for people to read. So it's very important to us to make sure we don't make our people have to work hard or get a headache in order to read our sales message. So going with larger typefaces is absolutely essential when you're doing marketing copy. Otherwise, you might as well be telling people, look, I want to sell you something, but I absolutely refuse to do that until you go get your glasses to read my promotion. So we make sure that in our promotions, which are largely the people average age 65 or older, that our body text is 12 to 13 point times Roman. And we've gone as high as 14 point. I have a supernet package where we used a courier face throughout that package because we wanted it to feel like a bullet and something had just been torn off a teletype. And we went with 14 point American typewriter in that. So you can go, if you're using a larger page size, you can go to 14 or 15 point and get away with it. And on the web, you'll notice that everything we do is 12 point aerial. 11 point for Dana is about the same. The aerial is a little more, the type is a little bit more extended in that the letters are broader. And so I think aerial is more readable than for Dana, but that's just me. I don't have any studies to bear that out. But these are good rules of thumb for type selection and printed on the web body text should be said in the face. It appears larger to the eye than nine point. That's your absolute minimum. The nine point times Roman. 12 to 13 is better for standard page sizes like 8 and a half by 11 pages. And if you're going with tabloid size pages or jumbo direct mail pieces, going to 14 or even 15 point is quite often desirable. And we covered the web thing. It amazes me that so many people go with the smaller typefaces drives me crazy. You have to wonder what they're thinking. Anyway, keep line length short. This is an actual direct mail piece that I received in the mail. This is what happens when a graphic artist doesn't understand what his job description is to grab attention and to promote readership. Your eye gets fatigued about here going across each line. And then once it gets here, it really doesn't know where to come back to over here. It's also a poor use of space. If these were columnated, you'd have more room on the page for more selling elements because you wouldn't have as much of this white space between lines. The white spaces would be short. So keep your line length short. Our rule of thumb is 35 to 38 or 39 characters per line. For optimum readership, it's not carved in stone. We're going to see some pieces where the line lengths are slightly longer, but this is just beyond the pale. This is a lesson in how to design copy that nobody can read or nobody will read. Again, it gets back to don't ask your prospect to do any work. Keep your paragraph short. I am guilty of breaking this rule from time to time. But the old rule in the old days when they did all of the readership studies was that five line paragraphs are the most readable. Don't go over five lines. That's very difficult to do sometimes. You may find yourself going, like in this paragraph here we go several more lines. But if you're making the effort to keep your paragraph short and throwing in some paragraphs shorter than five lines, just kind of fuzz your eyes and look at this page. The general feel of the page is that there's plenty of air. There's plenty of white space. So the page doesn't look intimidating even though the paragraphs are longer. Some of the paragraphs are longer. It's a good rule to keep in mind. Do it as much as possible. You'd be amazed when you go back through your copy. You type it out on a word form and you'll have a five line paragraph. But once they put it in the columns now it's 10 lines or 15 lines. And you'd be amazed when you look through your first draft of the graphics how easy it is to go through and pick points to break those paragraphs up. And once you've done that you get a page that looks much more inviting. Go with ragged write. This piece right here, the right hand edge of the column is justified. In order to do that they had to play all kinds of little games and tricks with the spacing between the words. That makes readership very, very difficult. If instead you go with a ragged write format, and this isn't mine, but because I think his letting here is a little tight, but if you instead go with ragged write you don't get those awkward little lines. And you get a much more interesting look to the page. We're going to mention this later, but I just want you to notice something on this page. I use it as a good example of ragged write. But notice the amount of space between the lines within a paragraph. It's very, very, very cramped. Typically, the other crime really is something like this where the letting is maybe a little bit too loose, a little bit too much. You need to find a happy balance between the two, so it's clear that the paragraph hangs together, but that the descenders from one line don't come close to crossing over the ascending characters from the line below it. This looks very dense just because of that letting between these lines. Typically, 12 on 12 is about the tightest you'd ever want to go. 12 on 13 would be about right, I would think, for most times faces. And it's much tighter than most graphics programs are set up to just automatically give you the text. They tend to give you much more letting than that. If I remember from Quark, it was like 12 would be on 14 points of letting or even 15. So keep an eye on that. You can go a long way to making pages more accessible. Error text out with what we call secondary letting. Secondary letting is a space between paragraphs. You'll notice you have your primary letting, which is a space between individual lines in the paragraph. The secondary letting is the space between the paragraphs. Now this piece here has no secondary letting at all. Each paragraph is sitting right on top of the one below it. And as a result, despite the indents, this looks like just one big long paragraph. You really have to want to know about the mega oil shock in order to wade through this text. On this page, on the other hand, you have nice secondary letting, appropriate letting within the paragraphs, some indented paragraphs to further break things up. And this is a good use of white space. Artists love white space for what they tend to do to give you white spaces. They'll give you all of this white, and then they'll give you little tiny text crammed together down here to preserve their white space up here. This is how white space should be used along copy, where it's impregnated into the text in order to make the page look more inviting. Begin paragraphs with a tab. That should be self-explanatory. You want to indent three to four characters on your tabs. More than that, just looks uncomfortable and cumbersome. Less than that, you might as well not have the indent. But again, this is another way of infusing a little bit of white space and making the page less painful to read. Notice how many of these things, we're just really talking paragraphs right now, but notice how many of these things have to do with subliminally communicating to your reader that, hey, not only is the text about an exciting subject, not only are the benefits great, not all this other stuff, this is going to be easy to read. This thing is going to read itself. Just come on down into the text and let's talk. There's a time argument for that. Everyone's busy. Nobody wants to read anything that looks like it's going to take them a long time. No one wants to read anything that looks like it's going to be work. I run into this all the time as one of my pet peas, and you guys have probably heard it before. I have artists who will take a subhead like this that's in the text, and they will actually run, I guarantee you'll be, part of the subhead at the bottom of a column, and then top of the column, the subhead resumes thrilled with the profits undiscovered stocks makes you. That's a no-no. You've got to keep your thoughts together. It takes some massaging and moving things around, but never break a subhead at all. When you break the lines in a subhead, do it intelligently. This is something my webguides around here get nailed for every single day, and we're going to stay on it until it doesn't happen anymore. A subhead is a sentence or a phrase, and the lines in the phrase need to be broken if it's a multi-line subhead, so that each line conveys a complete phrase or a complete thought. My favorite example is, you don't want to say, I want to kiss her, but she won't let me. So it's important to look at the phrasing on your subheads and how they're broken, so that to the scanner and the quick reader, the subhead communicates clearly and quickly. Widows and orphans can be really valuable to you as an artist. You're looking for a way to make sure that you're not going to be in a hurry. Widows, a single word on a line at the end of a paragraph, a widow, that's fine. That adds a little bit of white space to the page without allowing the page to lose its cohesiveness. Orphans, on the other hand, a single line of a paragraph, a single line of a paragraph, at the top or bottom of a column, should be avoided for that very reason. It slows the momentum of reading, and quite often, by the time the eye gets to the top of the next column, the brain will have forgotten what it just read at the bottom of the previous column. So, the first column, the first column, the first column, the first column, is the first column, the first column, the first column, the first column, has forgotten what it just read at the bottom of the previous column. So, again, look to break paragraphs where you have to break them from the bottom of the column to the top. Look to break them in ways that keep complete thoughts together. Never end a column or a page with a period. This has to do with graphics being used to keep people reading and keep turning pages. If at the bottom of a page you have a period, there's no reason to turn the page, keep reading. If, on the other hand, at the bottom of the column on the right-hand page, you say, and the most important thing you can do is, and it's not the end of the sentence, people are going to turn the page to find out what the most important thing to do is. A lot of this, I've done it quite often, will explicitly design front pages of promotions to break just that way, even if we have to pad it, even if we have to pad the promotion with graphics or whatever on page one so that we drive the text down so it breaks at a tantalizing place that makes you, you know, you just have to turn the page and read the rest of that sentence. As copywriters, we work our tails off to raise the emotional temperature of our prospects. We then hand the copy off to an artist, and the artist quite often will be the person who gets to choose the colors that will be used in that promotion. And the artist has a different agenda in many cases. The artist has to fit everything on the page. The artist, you know, has a lot of things that they have to do with this text. One of the things that they quite often will miss is the fact that we've worked hard to heat the prospect up. We don't want them to use cold colors to subliminally cool the prospect off. And so I tend to stay with earth tones, warm colors, burgundies, browns. There are warm greens. We have one here on this boardroom package I did. Nice warm green. Blue can be warm too if it's saturated enough, not this light baby blue like on this package. See, look at how cold this is. And think about another thing here. Not only are you subliminally cooling people off with these cold colors, but also what is the relative mailbox visibility of this piece versus this piece or this piece? If this came in the mail, it wouldn't jump to the top of the stack for you, but how could you not look at this? So stay with warm colors. Stay with vivid colors wherever appropriate. There'll be, of course, opportunities. If I was doing this and I wanted it to look like a special report, it might be displaying it, might not have any illustrations or anything like that on it. But the thing is that I wouldn't go with the blues. I would stay away from that and instead I would stick with warmer colors. I'm going to have to move a little quickly on the rest of our slides today. Time is getting away from us. A lot of these things are intuitive. Keep your backgrounds light in sidebars. Quite often you'll put it, but in running text you're going to see most good copywriters, most good mailers. We'll use black text on a white background, maybe a second color, a warm color, a red, or a burgundy, or a brown, or something for subheads and so forth. In the sidebars there's usually a screen behind the sidebar to differentiate it from the running text. Keep those screens very light. The principle here is expressed in this box. The greater the contrast, the humanized has been trained and brain has been trained to see contrast. So the greater the contrast is, the easier it is to read. Black on white is best, but as you lighten the text or darken the background, you lose readership. So I tend to stay with 10 or 15% screens behind text in sidebars if I have to have a screen, and I stay with black ink on those very light backgrounds. And the backgrounds can quite often, I try to keep them warm as too, light yellows, light beiges, manillas, and so forth. In consumer pieces I'll quite often go with blue backgrounds or green backgrounds, but again the overall impact of the sidebar needs to be warmth. So you avoid that subliminal cooling that can cost you sales. Make sure that you always include contact information on each spread of your piece. You never know when a prospect is going to be ready to order, and you want to be there with a toll-free number or with instructions on what to do next at any point during the delivery of the sales message. This is something that we copywriters can ask for in our text, but it's also something that good artists will know to ask the copywriter to give them or to make room for a header or a footer on every spread. Now on a lot of our pieces, in addition to this footer where we have the toll-free number that they can call, there may be an action device that also has a toll-free number. That's fine. The more times you can give it out, and the more places, the better off you're going to be. You never want your prospect to have to go looking for instructions on how to order. It needs to be in front of them all the time. I jokingly call this my law of space and time. Every element that makes it into the final design displaces another element that didn't. So when you look at any graphic element in a piece, you have to ask yourself, is this going to help you sell the product, or is this just somebody throwing something in for the heck of throwing it? The pieces you're looking at here, for example, are case in point. This cover of a promotion, I don't remember who did it, the artist felt compelled to create a border around his text. Now this is totally dead space. So that means that the client is spending good money to send green ink to a bunch of prospects that isn't going to sell doodly squat. And it can find his copy in a much smaller space and limits the amount of text he can use. This is the cover. So it limits the amount of space he can use to sell the prospect on reading this promotion. So if you're a prospect and you're just wishing that somebody would send you a bunch of green ink, you're going to love this piece. But if you're an investor and you're looking for analysis on how the Clinton presidency is going to affect your investments, you're forced down into this cramped copy with no letting between the paragraphs. And it's just a mindless decision to make, quite frankly. Another common mistake you're going to see is artists who will throw up barriers between columns of text and boxes around pages of text. And these things just take up space. The ultimate insult is to write your copy. Have the artist call you up and say, oh, the copy is four pages too long. And he said, well, send me your rough. And he sends you the rough. And he's got a box around every page. He's got vertical lines between the columns. He's thrown in all kinds of crap that doesn't need to be there. And now he wants you to cut copy to one thing that's actually going to sell the product. Well, as you can probably imagine, there are great screams of pain that happen around here when that suggestion is made to me. We just get rid of the extraneous stuff. Everything in that piece has to pull its own way. Choose the right eye candy. If you haven't read the Daniel Starch McGraw Hill readership study, I suggest you get ahold of it. It's quite old now, but human nature hasn't changed much. I think it came out in the late 70s, early 80s. But human nature hasn't changed a whole lot. And I would suspect that most of the things that study found were true. One of the things it found was that if you're marketing to women, women are going to want to see pictures of children and other women. Those are the things that draw their eye, catch their eye, grab their attention, draw them into the text. Guys don't even make the list. When you're marketing to men, on the other hand, we want to see women. We want to see exotic cars, boats, airplanes, helicopters, other modes of transportation. These two things are very close in terms of the amount of attention that they will draw from the man. The limits that are placed on you in terms of using these things are just dictated by your own creativity. Larry, the name of the study was the Daniel Starch Slash McGraw Hill Study. It's a readership study. It's fantastic. It gets into a lot of the things that we've covered today about typefaces, type style, paragraph formatting. I think there are a couple more slides containing information I took from that study. I read it 20-some-odd years ago, and I still implement those things every single day today. This is one of them. Try a storyboard. In marketing to both sexes, images that tell a story work best, like storyboards. You probably all remember those old Canadian Club ads. They were on the back of like every Life Magazine when I was a kid. Guy and a Girl Go Skying, Escape and Abel Edge, Pedestrian Bernard, they had a wonderful day. Then at the last frame of this little comic strip, there are actually photographs of real people, not illustrations, is they're sitting around a fire with a Canadian Club. That format produced the greatest readership of any ad format that was tested. We use storyboards in a lot of our health promotions, and they'll be an example of one of those coming up. This is another case of an artist feeling like they haven't really justified their income because they didn't throw enough stuff into the piece. Look here, this is a combination of the vertical barriers between columns, which force you to have a wider gutter, which limits the number of characters you can have per line, and therefore limits the amount of text in the piece. That wasn't enough for this artist. They also had to throw this meaningless photograph in. Illustrations are essential in good direct mail and good advertising, but when the illustration doesn't say anything or do anything or advance a sale, it's a waste of space. You're spending money to send ink to people where you could have used that space in a much more productive way. Again, Clayton's Law of Space and Time. This little globe here means that something else that might have convinced somebody to buy didn't get into the piece. It also makes it a little more difficult to read. This artist didn't even do a good job clipping around the illustrations, so the letters here are coming way too close to it and the ellipsis over here. Again, no nonsense. Take no prisoners. You're out to sell something. You're not out to make the world prettier by sending them a bunch of pretty pictures. Make eye contact. Using a graphic of your advocate, of your spokesperson, delivering an important component to your sales message actually in quotes saying something that is designed to either lend credibility, strengthen bonding, or actually sell the product is an extremely powerful way to use graphics in your promotions. You can use it to present a guarantee. You can use it to present a track record as we do here and here. These were three different financial promotions. We have been taught since we were little. You don't trust people who won't look you in the eye, so let's look them in the eye and tell them the truth. Deliver the good news about our product. Here are five more ways to power up your promotion with illustrations. By illustrations, by the way, I mean photographs, drawings, illustrations, charts, graphs, tables, any graphic element. First, you can seize prospects' attention and convert it to readership by including the spokesman's photograph with the headline. Now you've got a full brain headline with your spokesman establishing contact and delivering passion in his expression and his body language along with your headline. Very powerful. You see an awful lot of my clients doing that. Here we're establishing credibility using graphic elements. I hear we're actually using the storyboard concept on the front cover of our promotion. This promotion is mailed well over 30 million pieces for me so far. We have a new, you know, part two, a new package that I just beat this promotion with, but it took two years. At any rate, you'll notice how we use photograph of the customer along with this testimonial as the head. The photograph really makes the testimonial live, makes it feel real, credible. Then we have our storyboard showing how the product works. We show that the product is a harmless white powder and the copy bears it out. Then we have, you know, some lifestyle shots attached to testimonials here. So again, you're establishing credibility out the wazoo. We got to tell quite a bit of our story right on the cover. You can activate your prospects, dominant emotions. We mailed this right after 9-11. If you remember that time, we're talking about war with Afghanistan and there was anthrax in the mail. So those were anxious times. They were very, very, very tough times. So I found this stock photo and I talked about him before, but he was just perfect for delivering that whole brain. This is how we all feel right now, you know? And then with prescription roulette, we use the six-gun, so I feel lucky. So again, this is an effective use of a photograph or an illustration because it ties directly to the headline here and the text and amplifies it, intensifies it, dimensionalizes it. Here's our another storyboard. It's for the same product that this was internally. We show exactly how the product works. It's an extremely effective use of graphics. We show how the problem arose, how bad it could get, and you could have a heart attack in how the product works. This is from another piece that we did. I think Carleen was the primary writer on this one where we show how healthy acid-ph balance in your body promotes health and we actually show the process. Again, using the storyboard technique. You use them, as we've discussed many times before, to present the product in premium and enhance perceived value, dimension-wise value of your product. These pages typically face the order form page and you can see just the sheer value of everything you get. Getting pages turned is absolutely essential, obviously. I mean, we've talked about each paragraph. The headline sells the deck, sells me on reading the deck. The deck sells me on reading the opening copy. The opening paragraph sells me on reading paragraph two and so on and so on throughout the piece. But you have a challenge. Every two pages, they're going to have to turn the page. They're going to have to physically decide to turn the page of the piece. That's a great time to think about using a graphic of some kind, whether it's an arrow or a small box telling them what's on the next spread or some other device to get them to turn the page. A barrier to getting that done, and this is actually a right-hand page in a Magalock. A barrier to getting that done is placing full-size, full-height sidebars. I wish I had the other page of this. I just didn't include it, but this page over here was all text. This page here is a sidebar. This text page was a continuation of this longer article. There ran through the entire piece. The artist on this, and this was Bloomberg, so I had nothing to do with the graphics, Arbiter rarely chose to put a full-page sidebar on the right-hand page. So I'm reading, reading, reading your three-column text. Here I get to the bottom. It says, continue on page 18, and bang, here I have this. Now, I have to do something with this. Decide what I'm going to do with it, and then I have to decide if I'm going to change pages. It's been so easy simply to have this on the left-hand page. So if I wanted to, I could continue reading the text I was reading, and then still, you know, at the bottom of the right-hand page here be told, please turn. So keep your full-height sidebars. Think of them as Chinese walls. You don't want a Chinese wall here. You want to put that over here so that the running text is easy to follow. Let your illustrations point the way. This is quite simple. If you're using a person's face, have them face into the page or towards the copy. Their eyes are a pointer. You know how you walk in the street if everyone's looking up on the street corner, you stop and look up? Well, the same thing happens to your prospects when they're reading your promotion. So, you know, have your illustrations point to the copy you want the prospect to read. And you'd be amazed how many times artists will give you a piece where, you know, the subject of the photograph is facing away from the text you actually want the person to read. Never a good idea. This is where I've talked about this several times, but this is a good example of, you know, a readership device that just says turn the page now. You want to get people, you know, never forget that your job is to get people to turn that page at the bottom of every right-hand page. We're going to do a whole session on risk relief at some point, but, you know, making a guarantee is a contract with your prospect. It's a contract, and it should look like it's official. It should look like it's a contract or a certificate or something else, you know, that makes it real. So, graph, turning it into a certificate, as in this case with banknote borders, is one great way to do it. You can probably think of many other ways to do it. But, you know, you'll have your guarantee and you're running tax. There's no reason in the world. In fact, I almost always do this, you know, not to have the guarantee also set out as a standalone sidebar. In fact, I'll have it in the text, I'll have a standalone sidebar. I'll have another guarantee like this one presented on the page facing my order form or on the order form page, and then I'll reference the guarantee in the positive acceptance copy on the order form. You can never present that guarantee too much. And somebody was telling me the other day that headlines that use the word guarantee or refer to the guarantee are also very effective. Also, quite often we'll put bursts on our covers if we have a product first promotion where it's not an advertorial. It's obviously, it's coming to you and say, hey, I'm here to sell you something. You know, we have a USP or a benefit oriented headline. We'll quite often include a burst on the cover that says absolutely guaranteed to work or your money back. You know, so, you know, think about ways to graphically present that guarantee. Let's see here. We've got a couple of questions real quick. Chuck wants to know how I insert myself in the design process. I'll answer that at the end. Chuck, that's okay. Steve wants to know on the web, doesn't put in contact information. Click here on the first few pages asking for action before the prospect is officially warmed up. Like asking for a kiss before you even take her out to dinner. Yeah, Steve. The asking for the order both on the web and PG version, right? Asking for the order on the web or indirect mail prematurely is not a good idea. If you look at my swipe files, you'll notice that I tend to have my first action devices on page six or eight long after I've gone through the advertorial process, which is, you know, talking about the topic of the piece, delivering valuable information to demonstrate my expertise and to bond with the prospect. You know, I'm not mentioning a product yet in the text, so it would be crazy to have an order now box on those pages. We do the same thing on the web. If you look at our landing pages in our online store, everybody that sees those pages knows, hey, you know, I'm here to check out a product that I might buy, right? But if you look at the pages that we do for WISe research and Health Resources, you'll notice that they're advertorial in nature. And so the first order device doesn't occur in the ad until we've actually introduced the product and quite often also named the price. So, again, it's just common sense. You know, you're going into an advertorial saying, all right, well, I'm going to seduce my reader by offering him something free that has value, which is this advertorial. And I'm going to tell him how to have a greener lawn, and I'm going to give him seven points that he can take and use, whether he buys from me or not. That's what an advertorial is, right? And it's my way of demonstrating the value of what I do, and it's my way of, you know, showing him that I really have his interest at heart, and I'm not just another salesman. Okay, fine. So you don't, you know, just common sense would dictate then that you don't bring your product into the discussion, either with a graphic device or a text until you've accomplished those goals first. And typically that's in the first six or eight pages of 24-page mangalog. Let's see here. Okay, finishing up now. 28, use action devices to help prospects order when they're ready. We just addressed this. Now, to my own chagrin, as I was going through John Knudson and I were going through some of our sample files, I wasn't able to find as many promotions as I was hoping, where I did the brilliant thing, which was that when I have the action device, action devices on my spread, that I would have my toll-free number preeminent, you know, large, you know, $300 in free gifts and discounts, reverse heart disease in 90 days or less, dial toll-free 1-800-NOW, and then very small, or use the order form on page 23 of this report. I want people to use the phone. I don't want them to send that response device in. As I've said before, that's about the worst thing, the second worst thing you can do. The worst thing you can do as a prospect of mine is to throw away my piece and not buy. The second worst thing is to order by response device through the mail, because that means I don't have an opportunity to bump you on the phone. If you call the toll-free number to order, then I can have my operator scripted to explain to you how much money you save by taking my two-year offer instead of my one-year offer, or buying whatever my bump offer is, you know. So, you know, be clear-headed about what you want that prospect to do, and make sure these action devices focus on that. Now, this here is there by accident, because it's a footer. When this was written, and this isn't one of my pieces, but when this was written, this should have had the telephone number up in lights, maybe a graphic image of a telephone, it should have then had the telephone number itself really huge so I couldn't miss it, you know. And then it should have my action device copy, which is the argument for why I should call right now. But on both of these, we are doing more really to guide them to the order form than we are the phone number, which I think is a strategic blunder. Use images and bursts to convey product value. Bursts are hokey, and they work. And the only reason that we are able to wrestle our designers to the ground and get them to include bursts on pictures of products and premiums is because they work. So, you know, stand your ground, demand a burst. If he doesn't want to have a little seal with jagged edges, you know, go ahead and use a banner across, and make it, you know, a nice black box with yellow type or a nice white box with red type. But, you know, some kind of a device that conveys the value and the fact that it's free whenever you show a product. And if you're showing the totality of the offer, the product, the premiums and everything, add up all of the values and include that with it as well. Order devices can get unbelievably complicated. And this is an area where graphic artists and copywriters need to work together better. There are a series of compromises being made. Now, I think this is a great order device. You'll notice several things about it. First, although this was a very large piece, we've split this inside back page vertically. That's so that people can fax. This will fit through a fax machine. So, it facilitates fax ordering. They use this space here for the guarantee. That's cool. Over in here where you have the actual order, I have a visual representation of every free gift I get. When I subscribe to this newsletter. And very short copy about my best value. It tells me how much I save, how much I get. So, in that regard, I think this is a very well thought out, well executed order form. It's very easy to get too complicated and to make an order form look like it's just too hard to use. And for those, that percentage of people that you're talking to of your prospects, who will not order over the phone and who only will use an order form. If that order form is way too complex or intimidating, he's probably not going to order. So, you've lost a sale. Let me take a look at some of the questions we've gotten here and see if we can dispatch some of those. Well, Rick Dulema likes our Salvador Dolly picture at the beginning. Alright, that was just a thank you. I'll see. I answered Tony's question and Larry's. Bob wants to know why does all our stuff online and newsletters have gray padded sides? I assume you're talking about the emails that we send out. We use HTML to design our emails because that gives us greater trackability. But what happens is, depending on the person's screen size, the line length can get excessive. I've got a 32 inch monitor and two 24's and they're all panoramic. The aspect ratio is that they're very much wider than they are tall. So, when I open an email that isn't constrained in some way, I'll have a line of text running across my whole 32 inch screen. That's impossible to read. So, we constrain the line length by putting our emails in a format that is more the aspect ratio of a page. So, it's roughly, what would that be? I forget. It's something like 1.5 times taller than it is wide or something like that. But at any rate, it feels more like a printed page and it keeps the line length short enough to keep it readable. There's so much that you don't have control of on the web. That's one thing we can't control. Sean says, awesome call. He's got to run to deliver a coaching call. Okay, Sean. Good luck. Okay, Larry. What was the name of the study again? Daniel Starch McGraw-Hill. Covered that. Chuck. Oh, yeah. How do you insert yourself in the design process? When your client insists on sending a really vanilla sales letter. Well, I think for me, if I'm starting to work with a new client and make it very clear up front, here's what's going to happen. Okay? You're going to send me the research that I requested. I'm going to write a draft. You're going to comment on the draft. I will then decide which of your comments are valid and which ones I'm not going to address. And I'll send you a second draft. And we'll go back and forth like that until we have something that we both like. Remember, you're a partner with your client. If you're a copywriter. If he doesn't make money, you're not going to make money. You can't just sit idly by and watch any decision on any level, not just graphics, but changes to the offer, change the headline, any other change in the promotion to happen without your input, because he's spending your money if he's doing that. Because you're hoping for royalties, right? At the very least, you're hoping for repeat assignments. So I make it clear up front how the process is going to go. And every single client I've ever worked with is thrilled when I tell them that I want to be a part of the graphics process. They're looking for that kind of input. And if the client is kind of new to this stuff and a little naive, doesn't really know how to work with writers, he's going to like it because he's going to figure, hey, I'm getting something free here. I'm getting extra time from this guy to help with my graphics, and it's not going to cost me anything. If it's an experienced mailer, they're really going to like it because they know that the whole thing here is that for this to work, the copywriter's vision needs to be accomplished. And that doesn't just stop at the submission of your text. It goes on to how every page works, how every spread works, the placement of sidebars, and so forth. So I bet I spend a third of the time that I spend doing graphics direction, just moving sidebars around to make sure that they appear at the proper place in the piece appropriate to what's happening in the running copy at the time. So I don't think you'll get any kind of resistance on that one. People love it when the copywriter stays involved at that level. Okay, my thing's jumping around a little bit. Okay, Ahsoka has a great question. Do you find that different markets read copy and respond to design differently? In other words, would you use different colors and styles when writing the financial markets compared to the weight loss market or arthritis? The answer to that is absolutely, positively yes. Earlier in today's session we looked at a tabloid that was for a health product. And I talked about how we call it the biggest, that is happiest flusy in the biker bar because it's got very vivid colors and it's got a lot of lifestyle shots. There's a lot of visual emotion to it. That is a completely different look from what you use in the financial markets where your images are going to be tied much. You're not going to be using lifestyle shots of people jumping on the beach and stuff like that. You're going to be using charts and graphs judiciously to make your points and photographs of the product and premiums to communicate value. So yes, the markets respond very differently. In the health market you're talking to consumers. Everyone has a body. Everybody's got a heart they want to protect. Everyone's got eyes that they wish they could see better with. The products you're selling really are very broad based and the lists are very broad based that you're going to be addressing, the mailing list. In the financial it's much tighter. People tend to be a little more serious about their money than they are about topics like sex and blood pressure and things like that. At least in this regard, believe it or not. That gets back to what we were talking about at the beginning where the format needs to be appropriate for the market as well as for the message. All of the other things that you consider in choosing your format, the format then drives what kind of illustrations you use and so forth. But remember what we talked about, you're going to have to consider the cost, the turnaround time in production of the format, the market, the message. All of those things play into it. And of course there's another thing. You may say, well, just for the hell of it, not based on anything, but I think this message, I mean not based on any form or testing, but this particular message I think needs to be delivered on tape or on a CD or maybe in print, but instead of any of the formats we've looked at, maybe it needs to be a bundle of playing cards or something. Obviously if you decide to go with a format like that, the types of illustrations and so forth that you're going to use are going to have to adapt to that format. Having said that though, anytime you have any amount of copy that people are going to have to read, more than a paragraph or two, you're going to be deeply concerned with a lot of the things that we've talked about today including type selection, type face, secondary letting, indented paragraphs, iPad barriers and all of those other things. So let's, we have a few more. Steve wants to know if I have tips on finding a good graphic designer for the web. I don't. I'll tell you what I do. We tried a lot of graphic designers and web designers over time and with very, very poor results. And we have finally decided that the only way to do it is to hire good web designers and then bring them along in the graphic side of things. And we've done that. We have, I've got three guys sitting out there now. We're about to add a fourth. And this fourth one is going to be heavier in actual graphic design than he is in web programming and web design. So that gives us somebody with a good eye for color and a good eye for composition to compliment our other guys who have fantastic web design skills. How do I, Kim wants to know how I approach writing for a book log versus a mag log. Kim, typically what I do is I don't write book logs. Typically what I do is I write mag logs. And then I tend to break them down into book logs once they've been established as a control. But the difference with a book log is that you're essentially, you need to understand you're emulating a book. And so it's going to have front cover. It's going to have a liner notes about the author. It's going to have an inside title page. It's going to have an inside table of contents that may run two or four pages. Then it's going to have another title page for chapter one. And then your sales message is going to be broken up into chapters. So for example, on a current health promotion for a hard product, chapter one of my book log was about how to save your own life from a heart attack. And it basically talked about how the product does that. And chapter two was how to lower your blood pressure without drugs. Chapter two was about that. And chapter three was about how to lower your cholesterol. And chapter four was about lifestyle issues, you know, how to have more energy, how to do this, how to do the other thing. These chapters started out as an editorial and ended with documentation of how the, and testimonials of how the product accomplished those things. And then the final chapter was my offer chapter. And it was about how to save up to 75%, you know, on your health care costs. And it presented my offer, my discount, my premiums, and the whole nine yards. So that's usually it's very easy to take any kind of copy that's been written. And after the facts say, okay, I'm going to turn this into a book log. So I hope that answers your question. It shouldn't be that tough really. All right. Okay, good. Okay. Oh, Tony has one here. In some of your examples, a white font on a darker black background was used. The reverse type. Why is that acceptable? Tony, I did cover that earlier, but it's only acceptable to me personally. When the type is large enough that the reverse type prohibition really doesn't apply. I am 100% on board with the guys that say, if you run nine point times Roman and white on a black background, you're basically, no one's going to read it. I completely agree with that. But if I'm running 120 point impact in blazing yellow on a black background, I know that people are going to read that. You can't not read it. It's easy to read and it screams at you. Just as a fun little tip, we've even gone to five color printing. I like to go to the bookstore and stand in front of the magazine stand and kind of fuzz my eyes out and look at everything that's there on that stand. And I was doing that one day and I realized that all of the magazines that really jumped at me had day glow colors for their headlines. And it could be day glow green on a baby blue background, a lot of them are that way, or day glow orange on a black background. But that day glow really jumped. And so I started doing five color printing on my covers where I would use a day glow color for my main head and quite often reverse, quite often a lighter color than the background. So size matters. And you have to use your own judgment on that, but absolutely avoid using a white text on black backgrounds or light text on dark backgrounds for body text. Goodbye everybody. Thanks a lot.
